threats.title: AgentDAO Vacuous Truth: Zero TotalSupply Allows Unauthorized Proposal Execution
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentDAO.state() checks if forVotes == token().getPastTotalSupply(proposalSnapshot). When totalSupply at the snapshot block is 0 (no tokens delegated), this comparison yields 0==0==true, causing any Active proposal to return Succeeded. _tryAutoExecute then calls execute() atomically during the first For vote, or execute() can be called directly. An attacker who can create a proposal (via ContributionNft admin bypass or 0 threshold) can execute arbitrary actions on the DAO's assets without any real votes.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a logic error in the state override that creates a governance bypass. Any AgentDAO whose governance token has 0 delegated supply at a proposal's snapshot block is vulnerable. New agents or agents where tokens haven't been delegated are prime targets.

proof_of_signal.pattern: forVotes == token().getPastTotalSupply(proposalSnapshot(proposalId)) evaluates true when both are 0

preconditions: AgentDAO's governance token has 0 delegated supply at the snapshot block
Attacker can create a proposal (threshold == 0 or attacker is ContributionNft admin)
Proposal targets assets in the DAO's TBA or other controlled addresses

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Create proposal when no tokens are delegated; proposal auto-succeeds without any votes
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker drains all agent TBA assets via malicious proposal execution

economic_questions: How many AgentDAOs currently have 0 delegated supply at recent blocks?
What assets are held in agent TBAs that could be extracted?
Can the ContributionNft admin bypass be chained with this to create proposals on arbitrary agents?
validation_requirements: Verify getPastTotalSupply returns 0 for agents without delegations
Confirm execute() succeeds when state() returns Succeeded via 0==0 path
Test full attack chain: propose → wait → execute with 0 totalSupply

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal (only gas costs if threshold==0 or attacker is admin)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All assets in targeted agent TBAs
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Near infinite ROI (gas cost vs TBA assets)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after agent creation before tokens are delegated

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Agent token holders and TBA beneficiaries
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete loss of agent TBA assets
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire TBA holdings of vulnerable agents

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm 0==0 path in state() override bypasses quorum

threats.title: FERC20 burnFrom Does Not Decrement totalSupply Creating Supply Mismatch at Graduation
threats.hypothesis_description: FERC20.burnFrom and _burn reduce _balances[user] but do NOT decrement _totalSupply. During graduation, _openTradingOnUniswap passes _token.data.supply (original totalSupply) and tokenBalance to executeBondingCurveApplication. If the factory uses these to determine agent token allocation, the agent token supply is based on inflated FERC20 supply. Additionally, unwrapToken uses a 1:1 FERC20-to-agent-token mapping, but the pair may only hold lpSupply worth of agent tokens (tokenBalance at graduation), while users collectively hold S-tokenBalance FERC20 tokens. If more than half the supply was bought by users, there are insufficient agent tokens in the pair for all holders to unwrap.
threats.investigation_rationale: The burnFrom function explicitly reduces balance without reducing totalSupply, violating the ERC20 conservation invariant. This has downstream effects on graduation economics where supply parameters are derived from stale data.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _balances[user] -= amount without _totalSupply -= amount in both _burn and burnFrom

preconditions: Token graduates through bonding curve (gradThreshold reached)
Users hold more than half of FERC20 supply at graduation
AgentFactoryV3 allocates lpSupply tokens to vault and remainder elsewhere

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _balances[user] -= amount but _totalSupply unchanged; totalSupply() returns inflated value
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: At graduation, lpSupply=tokenBalance/1e18. If users hold S-tokenBalance > tokenBalance, pair has insufficient agent tokens for unwrap
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Early unwrappers get their tokens; late unwrappers get nothing (first-come-first-served insolvency)

economic_questions: What does AgentFactoryV3.executeBondingCurveApplication do with totalSupply vs lpSupply tokens?
At typical graduation points, what fraction of supply is held by users vs pair?
Does the inflated totalSupply affect maxTx calculation?
validation_requirements: Verify _totalSupply is not decremented in burnFrom/burn
Trace agent token allocation in AgentFactoryV3 for vault vs other recipients
Calculate expected tokenBalance at gradThreshold

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: No attack needed — affects all graduated tokens
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: First unwrappers drain pair's agent tokens; latecomers lose entire position
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: 100% for early callers of unwrapToken vs 100% loss for late callers

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Race condition for unwrap creates panic selling

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: FERC20 holders who don't unwrap quickly
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete loss of agent token claim
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: (S - tokenBalance - tokenBalance) worth of agent tokens if more than half supply is with users

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: gradThreshold determines token balance ratio at graduation
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify factory behavior with totalSupply and lpSupply parameters

threats.title: unwrapToken Lacks Access Control Enabling Forced Token Conversion Without User Consent
threats.hypothesis_description: Bonding.unwrapToken() is a public function with zero access control. Anyone can call it with any list of account addresses to forcefully burn their FERC20 tokens and transfer agent tokens to them. Since Bonding is the FERC20 owner, burnFrom(onlyOwner) succeeds. This removes user choice about when or whether to convert, and combined with the totalSupply bug (ECON-H002), creates a race condition where the first caller of unwrapToken for all accounts could be an attacker strategically converting specific users first to exhaust the pair's agent token allocation.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function performs an irreversible economic transformation on users' assets without their consent or any access control gate, violating the principle that users should control their own asset conversions.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Public function with no access control modifier, burns user tokens and transfers replacements

preconditions: Token has graduated (tradingOnUniswap == true)
Target accounts hold FERC20 tokens
Pair has sufficient agent token balance for the targeted accounts

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No msg.sender check, no acc == msg.sender check — anyone can trigger for any account
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls unwrapToken for specific accounts first to drain pair's agent token balance, then remaining holders can't unwrap
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: If pair has limited agent tokens, strategic unwrapping order determines who gets tokens vs who loses

economic_questions: Can the attacker profit by strategically ordering unwrap calls?
Does the pair have enough agent tokens for all holders?
Could forced conversion during unfavorable market conditions harm users?
validation_requirements: Confirm no access control on unwrapToken
Test forced conversion for accounts that didn't consent
Verify agent token balance in pair vs total FERC20 held by users

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.01 ETH gas for batch unwrap call
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on agent token scarcity in pair — could be significant if combined with ECON-H002
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Variable — high if agent tokens are scarce

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Forced mass conversion could move agent token price

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All FERC20 holders of graduated tokens
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Forced conversion without consent; potential loss if agent tokens insufficient
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire FERC20 market cap of graduated tokens

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Number of graduated tokens and holder counts
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare pair agent token balance vs sum of FERC20 holder balances

threats.title: AgentTax.dcaSell Incorrect Slippage Calculation Uses Input Amount Instead of Expected Output
threats.hypothesis_description: dcaSell calculates minOutput as (amountToSwap * (DENOM - slippage)) / DENOM, where amountToSwap is in taxToken units. However, minOutput is the minimum acceptable output in assetToken. This assumes a 1:1 exchange rate between taxToken and assetToken. If taxToken is worth significantly less than assetToken, minOutput is astronomically higher than any realistic output, causing all swaps to revert (permanent DoS on tax distribution). If taxToken is worth more, minOutput is near zero, providing no slippage protection.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a formula error in the slippage protection mechanism. The correct formula should use router.getAmountsOut to get expected output, then apply slippage tolerance to that. Using input amount as basis for output minimum is mathematically incorrect for any non-1:1 price pair.

proof_of_signal.pattern: minOutput = ((amountToSwap * (DENOM - slippage)) / DENOM) uses input-denominated value as output minimum

preconditions: taxToken and assetToken have different market prices (virtually always true)
EXECUTOR_ROLE calls dcaSell with any slippage value

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: If taxToken=0.001 assetToken: minOutput = 0.99*amountToSwap >> actual expected output of 0.001*amountToSwap → always reverts (DoS)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Scenario 1: DoS preventing tax distribution. Scenario 2: If taxToken > assetToken, minOutput near 0 allows sandwich extraction
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: DoS scenario locks all accumulated tax revenue in the contract; sandwich scenario extracts up to 100% of swap value

economic_questions: What is the actual price ratio between taxToken and assetToken?
How much tax revenue is accumulated and at risk?
Does handleAgentTaxes (which uses router.getAmountsOut correctly) mitigate this for that path?
validation_requirements: Compare minOutput calculation vs actual expected output for known token pairs
Test dcaSell with realistic token prices
Verify if handleAgentTaxes path is sufficient backup

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A for DoS; sandwich requires flash loan capital
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: DoS: all accumulated tax revenue locked. Sandwich: up to 100% of swap value

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Agent creators waiting for tax distributions
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of all tax revenue from dcaSell path

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: taxToken/assetToken price ratio, accumulated tax balances
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compute minOutput vs expected output for production token pair

threats.title: Bonding.launch() Permanently Broken Due to Double NonReentrant Guard
threats.hypothesis_description: Bonding.launch() calls launchFor() internally, but both functions carry the nonReentrant modifier from ReentrancyGuardUpgradeable. When launch() enters the guard (status=ENTERED), the internal call to launchFor() attempts to re-enter the same guard and reverts. This permanently breaks launch(), forcing all token launches through launchFor() which accepts an arbitrary creator address parameter. Anyone can attribute token creation to any address, affecting who receives graduation rewards and AgentTax creator fee distributions (99% of tax revenue by default).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a clear code defect. The launch() function can never succeed. The economic impact is that launchFor() allows any caller to set any creator address, enabling creator attribution manipulation that redirects significant fee revenue.

proof_of_signal.pattern: launch() has nonReentrant and calls launchFor() which also has nonReentrant — nested guard always reverts

preconditions: Anyone can call launchFor() with arbitrary creator parameter
Token graduates and creator receives tax revenue

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Both launch() and launchFor() have nonReentrant from same ReentrancyGuardUpgradeable inheritance
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker sets creator=attacker_address in launchFor() for a token they expect to be popular, capturing 99% of future tax distributions
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: 99% of all agent tax revenue for that token goes to attacker-controlled creator address

economic_questions: How much tax revenue does a typical graduated agent generate?
Can creator be changed after launch via AgentTax.updateCreator?
Are there active tokens launched via launchFor with incorrect creator attribution?
validation_requirements: Confirm launch() reverts with double nonReentrant
Test launchFor() with arbitrary creator address
Trace creator address through graduation to tax distribution

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: purchaseAmount (fee + initial buy) in asset tokens
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 99% of all future agent tax distributions for the launched token
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on token popularity — potentially thousands of % over time

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When launching tokens expected to become popular
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Minimal market impact; social engineering impact on perceived creator identity

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Legitimate token creators who lose creator attribution
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative tax revenue for affected agents

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify launch() always reverts; verify creator propagation to AgentTax

threats.title: Deliberate VoteType Bypass Allows Strategic Double-Voting in Governance
threats.hypothesis_description: In GovernorCountingSimpleUpgradeable._countVote, VoteType.Deliberate (support=3) returns early without setting hasVoted[account]=true. A voter can first cast a Deliberate vote (which emits VoteCast with their full weight, revealing their voting power and position), then later cast an actual For/Against/Abstain vote. The first vote doesn't count toward tallies but reveals the voter's weight. The voter gains strategic advantage by observing other votes before committing. In AgentDAO, this is particularly impactful as the _castVote override checks hasVoted for score and maturity updates.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code explicitly returns early for Deliberate without setting the hasVoted flag, creating a bypass. This affects both GovernorCountingSimpleUpgradeable and GovernorCountingSimple, used in AgentDAO and VirtualGenesisDAO/VirtualProtocolDAO respectively.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (support == uint8(VoteType.Deliberate)) { return; } -- returns BEFORE setting hasVoted[account] = true

preconditions: Voter holds governance tokens with delegated voting power
Active proposal exists in any Governor using these counting modules

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Deliberate return bypasses hasVoted[account] = true on line 72, allowing subsequent real vote
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Voter reveals weight via Deliberate, monitors other votes, then strategically places real vote to swing outcome
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Strategic voting advantage in governance outcomes affecting treasury, rewards, agent upgrades

economic_questions: How much economic value is controlled by governance decisions?
Can this be used to game AgentDAO maturity scoring?
Does the VoteCast event for Deliberate mislead off-chain governance systems?
validation_requirements: Confirm Deliberate vote doesn't set hasVoted
Test sequence: castVote(Deliberate) → castVote(For) succeeds
Verify VoteCast events emitted for both calls

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Existing governance token holdings (no additional capital)

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All governance participants in affected DAOs
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Treasury and agent assets controlled by governance

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Number of active governance proposals and stakes

threats.title: Genesis Per-Transaction Contribution Cap Bypass via Repeated Participation
threats.hypothesis_description: Genesis.participate() checks virtualsAmt <= maxContributionVirtualAmount per transaction, but does not enforce a cumulative per-user cap. The check on line 157 only validates the current transaction amount, not mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] + virtualsAmt. A whale can call participate() repeatedly, each time contributing the maximum per-tx amount, to accumulate an arbitrarily large position. This undermines the intended fair distribution cap.
threats.investigation_rationale: The variable name 'maxContributionVirtualAmount' and the per-tx check suggest the design intent is to limit individual contributions. The missing cumulative check is a logic error that enables cap bypass.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(virtualsAmt <= maxContributionVirtualAmount) checks single tx only, not mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] + virtualsAmt

preconditions: Genesis is in Active state (started, not ended, not failed/cancelled)
User has sufficient VIRTUAL tokens for multiple contributions

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] += virtualsAmt with only per-tx limit, not cumulative
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Call participate(1, maxContributionVirtualAmount) N times to contribute N * maxContributionVirtualAmount
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Whale dominates genesis allocation, receiving disproportionate agent tokens during distribution

economic_questions: What is the typical maxContributionVirtualAmount vs total genesis target?
How are agent tokens distributed relative to virtual contributions?
Does the off-chain system account for over-contributions?
validation_requirements: Confirm multiple participate() calls succeed for same user
Calculate maximum possible over-contribution
Verify distribution formula in onGenesisSuccess

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Disproportionate share of agent token allocation
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on genesis popularity and agent token value

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Fair distribution value of genesis agent tokens

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test repeated participate() calls and resulting allocation

threats.title: Bonding.setFee Missing Ether Scaling Creates Fee Miscalculation
threats.hypothesis_description: In Bonding.initialize(), fee is calculated as `(fee_ * 1 ether) / 1000`, applying ether scaling. However, setFee() directly assigns `fee = newFee` without the same scaling. If the owner calls setFee intending the same semantics as initialization (e.g., passing 5 to mean 0.5%), the fee becomes 5 wei instead of 5e15 wei. This makes the fee requirement `purchaseAmount > fee` trivially satisfied, collecting essentially zero fees. Conversely, if the owner passes an already-scaled value, the comparison works but there's no guard or documentation enforcing this.
threats.investigation_rationale: The asymmetric scaling between initialize and setFee is a code defect. The setter doesn't apply the same transformation as the initializer, creating a maintenance trap that can lead to zero or incorrect fees.

proof_of_signal.pattern: initialize: fee = (fee_ * 1 ether) / 1000; but setFee: fee = newFee (no scaling)

preconditions: Owner calls setFee with unscaled value (intending same semantics as initialize)
Or owner calls setFee with any value — no validation on range

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: initialize: fee = (5 * 1e18) / 1000 = 5e15. setFee(5): fee = 5 (5 wei). 1 million X difference.
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: If fee becomes ~0, all launches pay essentially no fee. If fee becomes huge, launches become impossible (DoS).
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Zero fee means protocol loses all launch fee revenue; excessive fee creates launch DoS

economic_questions: Has setFee been called in production? With what values?
What is the expected fee revenue from token launches?
Could this also affect the fee recipient address?
validation_requirements: Compare fee values after initialize vs after setFee with same intended percentage
Test launchFor with fee=5 wei (effectively zero fee)

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: N/A — affects protocol revenue, not direct exploit
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Protocol loses fee revenue on all future launches

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Current fee value on-chain, setFee call history
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare initialize scaling vs setFee direct assignment

threats.title: AgentDAO.getMaturity Division By Zero Bricks Service NFT Minting
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentDAO.getMaturity() computes `_proposalMaturities[proposalId] / forVotes` without checking for forVotes == 0. If a proposal reaches Succeeded state with 0 for-votes (possible via the 0 totalSupply bug in ECON-H001 or other edge cases), calling getMaturity reverts. ServiceNft.mint() calls getMaturity during the minting process, so any proposal that reached execution without real for-votes permanently blocks the contribution → service NFT pipeline for that proposal.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a division-by-zero in a function called within a state-changing transaction path (ServiceNft.mint), not just a view function. When combined with the vacuous success bug (ECON-H001), this creates a cascading failure.
preconditions: Proposal reaches Succeeded/Executed state with 0 for-votes
ServiceNft.mint() is called for this proposal

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Exploiting vacuous success (ECON-H001) to create executed proposals with 0 for-votes, then attempting to mint ServiceNft permanently fails
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: DoS on contribution pipeline for affected agents; may block legitimate model/dataset updates

economic_questions: How many proposals could have 0 for-votes at execution?
What is the economic impact of blocked ServiceNft minting?
Can this cascade to block all contributions for an agent?
validation_requirements: Confirm division by zero revert path
Test ServiceNft.mint with a 0-forVotes proposal
Verify no fallback handling exists

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal (gas for proposal creation and execution)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Combined with ECON-H001 for maximum impact

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Future contribution rewards and agent development value

threats.title: Genesis.onGenesisSuccess Repeatable Invocation Overwrites Unclaimed Token Distributions
threats.hypothesis_description: Genesis.onGenesisSuccess lacks a terminal success state flag. After the first successful call (agentTokenAddress set, claimableAgentTokens populated), the function can be called again by FACTORY_ROLE. The second call passes all modifiers (whenNotCancelled, whenNotFailed, whenEnded) and the refundUserCountForFailed==0 check. Since claimableAgentTokens uses `=` assignment (not `+=`), the second call overwrites unclaimed allocations with new values. OPERATION_ROLE can redirect agent tokens from legitimate users to attacker-controlled addresses.
threats.investigation_rationale: The absence of a success-terminal guard combined with overwrite (not accumulate) semantics creates a code-level vulnerability. While exploitation requires OPERATION_ROLE, this is a code bug — the function should not be callable twice.
preconditions: OPERATION_ROLE calls onGenesisSuccess a second time
Users from first distribution have not yet claimed
Second call provides different distribution amounts

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: First call distributes fairly. Second call sets allocations to 0 or redirects to controlled addresses.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker-controlled addresses receive agent tokens meant for legitimate participants

economic_questions: What is the typical delay between distribution and user claims?
How much value is at risk in agent tokens per genesis event?
Is there any off-chain monitoring for duplicate calls?
validation_requirements: Confirm second onGenesisSuccess call succeeds after first
Verify claimableAgentTokens overwrite behavior
Test claim after overwrite

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All unclaimed agent tokens from genesis

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Targeted theft of specific genesis allocations

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All genesis participants who haven't claimed
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete loss of agent token allocation
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire agent token distribution of affected genesis

threats.title: Genesis.withdrawLeftAssetsAfterFinalized Can Drain Unclaimed Agent Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: withdrawLeftAssetsAfterFinalized allows DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE to withdraw ANY ERC20 token from the Genesis contract after finalization, including the agent token backing unclaimed user distributions. The function only checks IERC20(token).balanceOf(this) >= amount, with no reservation for claimableAgentTokens obligations. Admin can withdraw all agent tokens, causing subsequent claimAgentToken calls to revert with insufficient balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function lacks accounting for committed liabilities. While admin-only functions are trusted, this specific function should not be able to withdraw assets that back user claims — this is a code defect in the protection of user funds.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No check against sum(claimableAgentTokens) — admin can withdraw tokens backing user claims

preconditions: DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE (FGenesis, controlled by ADMIN_ROLE) calls withdrawal
Genesis is finalized (ended + success/failed/cancelled)
Users have unclaimed agent tokens

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: require(amount <= IERC20(token).balanceOf(address(this))) — no subtraction of committed claimable amounts
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin calls withdrawLeftAssetsAfterFinalized(to, agentTokenAddress, fullBalance) draining all agent tokens
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Admin extracts all unclaimed agent token value

economic_questions: What is the typical unclaimed agent token value per genesis?
How long do users typically take to claim?
Is the admin address controlled by a multisig?
validation_requirements: Confirm withdrawal of agent tokens succeeds even when claimable balances exist
Test claimAgentToken after admin withdrawal
Verify admin trust model (multisig, timelock)

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete loss of agent token allocation
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Sum of all unclaimed agent tokens

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Genesis finalization status and unclaimed balances
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test withdrawal of agent token when claimable > 0
